title: Utility-first philosophy
question: What is utility-first CSS?
answer: Compose styles from small single-purpose utility classes directly in markup instead of writing custom CSS.
explanation: Tailwind removes context switches, keeps designs consistent via a design system config, and generates only the CSS you use.
codeExample: <button class="rounded-xl bg-indigo-600 px-4 py-2 text-white hover:bg-indigo-500">Save</button>
bestPractices: Extract components via @apply or React components once patterns repeat.
commonMistakes: Fighting utilities with lots of custom CSS.
interviewTips: Discuss constraints from tailwind.config.ts.

title: Responsive Modifiers
question: How do responsive prefixes work in Tailwind?
answer: Prefixes like sm:, md:, lg: apply the following utility only at that breakpoint and up (mobile-first).
explanation: Combine with dark:, hover:, focus:, group-hover: for state variations.
codeExample: ...
bestPractices: Start unprefixed for mobile, then layer breakpoints up.
commonMistakes: Using max-* variants unnecessarily.
interviewTips: Mention container queries (@container) in v3.2+.

title: Dark Mode
question: How do you enable dark mode in Tailwind?
answer: Set darkMode: 'class' in the config, then toggle the `dark` class on <html>. Use dark: variants in markup.
explanation: The 'media' strategy uses prefers-color-scheme automatically but can't be user-toggled.
codeExample: <html class="dark"><body class="bg-white dark:bg-slate-950">...</body></html>
bestPractices: Persist choice in localStorage.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to include dark utilities in the safelist for dynamic classes.
interviewTips: Discuss FOUC prevention with an inline script.

title: @apply and extracting components
question: When should you use @apply?
answer: Use @apply in CSS to bundle utilities into a semantic class when a pattern repeats in many places or in third-party markup you can't easily change.
explanation: Overusing @apply defeats the purpose of utility-first.
codeExample: .btn-primary { @apply rounded-xl bg-indigo-600 px-4 py-2 text-white; }
bestPractices: Prefer components in React/Vue over @apply when possible.
commonMistakes: Extracting single-use utility combos.
interviewTips: Show composing via React components as an alternative.

title: JIT Engine
question: What does Tailwind's JIT engine do?
answer: It generates CSS on demand by scanning content files, so any arbitrary value (e.g. `mt-[13px]`) works and bundles stay tiny.
explanation: Since v3, JIT is the only engine. It also enables faster builds.
bestPractices: Configure the content array carefully.
commonMistakes: Dynamic class names not being detected — safelist them.
interviewTips: Explain arbitrary variants and values.

title: Theme configuration and extend
question: How do you customize the design system using theme.extend?
answer: Add keys under theme.extend in tailwind.config.js to augment the default theme (colors, spacing, fonts) without overwriting Tailwind's defaults.
explanation: Values placed directly under theme (not extend) replace the defaults entirely, so use extend to add new tokens while keeping the built-in scale available.
bestPractices: Prefer theme.extend over theme to keep defaults.
commonMistakes: Overwriting theme.colors and losing built-in palettes.
interviewTips: Explain the difference between theme and theme.extend.

title: Arbitrary values
question: What are arbitrary values and when should you use them?
answer: Arbitrary values use square-bracket syntax like w-[347px] or bg-[#1da1f2] to produce a one-off utility that isn't in your theme scale.
explanation: They are ideal for exact values that don't warrant a theme token, but heavy use signals you should add a token to the config instead.
codeExample: Precise layout
bestPractices: Promote repeated arbitrary values into theme.extend tokens.
commonMistakes: Using spaces inside brackets — use underscores instead.
interviewTips: Mention arbitrary properties like [mask-type:luminance].

title: State variants: hover, focus, group, peer
question: How do group and peer variants differ from hover and focus?
answer: hover:/focus: style an element on its own state; group-* styles a child based on a parent marked with `group`; peer-* styles a sibling based on a preceding element marked with `peer`.
explanation: group and peer enable parent/sibling relational styling without JavaScript, useful for cards, form validation, and custom controls.
codeExample: Card title
bestPractices: Use named groups (group/item) when nesting groups.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to add the `group` or `peer` marker class.
interviewTips: Explain peer only affects following siblings.

title: Spacing scale
question: How does Tailwind's spacing scale work?
answer: Spacing utilities (p-*, m-*, gap-*) map to a numeric scale where each unit equals 0.25rem, so p-4 is 1rem and space-y-2 is 0.5rem gaps.
explanation: The consistent scale enforces rhythm across margin, padding, width, height, gap, and inset utilities from a single source of truth.
codeExample: Consistent spacing
bestPractices: Use space-* or gap for consistent child spacing.
commonMistakes: Mixing arbitrary px values instead of the scale.
interviewTips: Note 1 unit = 0.25rem = 4px by default.

title: Flexbox and grid utilities
question: How do you build layouts with Tailwind flex and grid utilities?
answer: Use flex with justify-*/items-* for one-dimensional layouts and grid with grid-cols-*/gap-* for two-dimensional layouts.
explanation: Tailwind mirrors CSS layout properties as utilities, so you compose alignment and distribution declaratively in markup.
codeExample:
Logo
Links
Cards
bestPractices: Use gap utilities instead of margins between flex/grid children.
commonMistakes: Forgetting flex/grid on the container itself.
interviewTips: Contrast justify (main axis) with items (cross axis).

title: Container and max-width
question: How do the container class and max-width utilities control content width?
answer: The container utility sets width to the current breakpoint's max-width; max-w-* utilities cap width to fixed sizes like max-w-3xl for readable content.
explanation: Unlike Bootstrap, Tailwind's container is not centered or padded by default — configure center and padding in theme.container.
codeExample: Readable content column
bestPractices: Configure container center and padding in the theme.
commonMistakes: Assuming container auto-centers without mx-auto.
interviewTips: Mention max-w-prose for text measure.

title: Official plugins: typography and forms
question: What do the @tailwindcss/typography and forms plugins provide?
answer: The typography plugin adds `prose` classes for styling rendered HTML/markdown; the forms plugin normalizes and base-styles form controls for easy utility overrides.
explanation: Plugins register new utilities and base styles via the plugins array, extending Tailwind beyond its core set.
bestPractices: Use prose for CMS/markdown output, not app UI.
commonMistakes: Applying prose to entire app and fighting its styles.
interviewTips: Mention prose modifiers like prose-lg and prose-invert.

title: Custom colors and design tokens
question: How do you define custom colors and design tokens with CSS variables?
answer: Define brand colors in theme.extend.colors, optionally referencing CSS variables so tokens can change at runtime for theming.
explanation: Using rgb(var(--color) / <alpha-value>) lets one color definition support opacity utilities while enabling runtime theme switching.
bestPractices: Expose colors as CSS variables for runtime theming.
commonMistakes: Hardcoding hex values scattered across markup.
interviewTips: Explain the <alpha-value> placeholder for opacity.

title: Content configuration and safelist
question: Why do dynamically generated classes get purged and how do you fix it?
answer: Tailwind only generates classes it finds as complete strings in the content files; concatenated class names aren't detected and get purged, so add them to safelist or use full literal strings.
explanation: The content array drives class detection. Building names like `bg-${color}-500` at runtime yields no match, so those styles are missing in production.
bestPractices: Map to full class strings instead of concatenating fragments.
commonMistakes: Building class names dynamically with template literals.
interviewTips: Explain safelist patterns with regex.

title: Transitions and animations
question: How do you add transitions and keyframe animations in Tailwind?
answer: Use transition, duration-*, and ease-* utilities for property transitions, and animate-* utilities (spin, ping, pulse, bounce) or custom keyframes in the config for animations.
explanation: Custom animations are defined under theme.extend.keyframes and theme.extend.animation, then applied via animate-<name>.
bestPractices: Respect motion-reduce with motion-safe/motion-reduce variants.
commonMistakes: Transitioning all properties, hurting performance.
interviewTips: Show defining custom keyframes in the config.

title: Transform utilities
question: How do transform utilities like scale, rotate, and translate work?
answer: Utilities such as scale-105, rotate-3, translate-x-4, and skew-y-2 apply CSS transforms and compose together via CSS variables.
explanation: Transforms are GPU-accelerated and commonly paired with transition and hover variants for interactive micro-effects.
bestPractices: Combine transform with will-change sparingly for heavy animations.
commonMistakes: Expecting transforms to affect document layout flow.
interviewTips: Note transforms can be composed on one element.

title: Prose typography styling
question: How does the prose class style long-form content?
answer: The prose class from the typography plugin applies sensible defaults to raw HTML like headings, paragraphs, lists, and code, so rendered markdown looks polished.
explanation: Prose modifiers (prose-lg, prose-invert, prose-headings:*) let you scale and theme the typography, including for dark mode.
codeExample:
Title

Rendered markdown gets beautiful defaults.
bestPractices: Use prose-invert for dark mode content.
commonMistakes: Applying prose to interactive app UI.
interviewTips: Mention element modifiers like prose-a:text-brand.

title: Important and specificity
question: How do you handle specificity and the important modifier in Tailwind?
answer: Prefix a utility with ! (like !text-red-500) to mark it important, or set important: true / a selector string in the config to boost all utilities against third-party CSS.
explanation: Because utilities share equal specificity, source order decides conflicts; important helps override external styles without restructuring markup.
bestPractices: Scope important with a selector to avoid global !important.
commonMistakes: Overusing ! and creating override wars.
interviewTips: Explain how utility order resolves conflicts.

title: tailwind-merge and clsx
question: Why use tailwind-merge and clsx together in component libraries?
answer: clsx conditionally joins class names while tailwind-merge intelligently dedupes conflicting Tailwind utilities so the last one wins, preventing broken overrides.
explanation: When passing className props, naive concatenation leaves both px-2 and px-4, and CSS order — not prop order — decides. tailwind-merge resolves this predictably.
bestPractices: Expose a cn() helper for all components accepting className.
commonMistakes: Assuming later class in string always wins without twMerge.
interviewTips: Explain why CSS order beats string order.

title: Tailwind v4 changes
question: What changed in Tailwind CSS v4?
answer: v4 introduces a CSS-first config using @theme in CSS, a high-performance Oxide engine, automatic content detection, and native cascade layers, reducing reliance on tailwind.config.js.
explanation: Instead of a JS config, you define design tokens directly in CSS with @theme, and the import simplifies to a single @import "tailwindcss" line.
bestPractices: Adopt CSS-first @theme tokens for new v4 projects.
commonMistakes: Expecting v3 JS config behavior to fully carry over.
interviewTips: Mention the Oxide engine and automatic content detection.
